turns.text: Hello Chanell, thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I'm a researcher studying maternity care experiences, and I'm really interested in hearing your personal story. I understand you're in the middle of your pregnancy, and I'd love to hear about your experience in your own words. There are no right or wrong answers — I'm just here to listen and learn from you. Everything you share will help us understand how to make maternity care better. Shall we begin?

It’s a strange thing, isn't it? Being on this side of the exam table. For years, I was the one wearing the coat, the one who had to hold it together while parents processed news that would change their entire lives. I thought I knew how this went—the screenings, the anatomy scans, the rhythm of the visits. But being thirty-six and staring at my own patient portal? It’s a different kind of vertigo. 

I’m seven pregnancies deep, and you’d think I’d be a pro by now, but the knowledge I have is a double-edged sword. Every time the sonographer goes quiet to focus on a measurement, my mind immediately jumps to the rare microdeletions I used to spend my days identifying in labs. It’s hard to just be a mother when your brain is hardwired to look for the "what ifs."

I appreciate you asking, though. Sometimes, navigating this alone—driving back to Gardner after an appointment in that heavy silence—it gets to be a lot. I’m just trying to learn how to trust the heartbeat instead of questioning the data. I’m ready to talk, but please, bear with me if I struggle to switch gears. It’s a work in progress. Where would you like to start?

When you first found out you were pregnant, what did you expect your first antenatal appointments to be like — the environment, the people, the information you'd receive? And what were they actually like?

Honestly, I think I walked in with a bit of a defensive wall up. Because of my background, I expected to be treated like a colleague, almost—you know, someone who would want the jargon, the stats, and the "why" behind every test. I expected a sterile, efficient transaction. I think, in a way, I *wanted* that because it felt safer. If I could keep it technical, I could keep it at arm's length.

But the reality was... softer, and that actually made it harder for me. My OB is wonderful, but she caught on early that I was over-analyzing. There was one appointment where I started firing off questions about the specific false-positive rates of the NIPT, trying to intellectualize my anxiety. She just put a hand on my arm and said, "Chanell, for today, let’s just look at this heartbeat." 

It caught me off guard. It made me realize that I was bracing for a medical consultation, but what I really needed was someone to reassure me that I was a mother-to-be, not just a high-risk chart. It wasn't the clinical environment I feared; it was the vulnerability of finally letting myself hope that everything would be normal. It’s a very strange friction to navigate—wanting to know everything while being terrified of what that knowledge might hold.
Before your screening tests and scans, what did you expect the process of understanding your results to be like — how information would be given, by whom, and in what way? And what was the reality?

I’ll be honest, I expected a phone call. In my old professional life, that’s how we did it—the call, the "don't panic, but we need to discuss further testing" speech. I think I had pre-scripted how I would handle that conversation. I had my "doctor face" ready, the one that stays calm and asks about mosaicism or penetrance while the world ends.

But the reality was much more isolating: it was a notification pinging on my phone while I was sitting on my couch in Gardner. It was just an update in the patient portal. You click the link, and suddenly you’re staring at a PDF that tells you if your child’s genetic future looks like a probability curve or a reality. 

There’s no "who" in that moment. There’s no one to read your tone or see your hands shaking. It’s just you and a digital wall of data. My medical training didn't prepare me for the sheer, cold silence of those results loading on a screen. I felt a weird mix of relief that I could interpret the assay and a profound, aching loneliness that I had to be the one to interpret it for myself. I didn't want to be the expert anymore; I just wanted to be a person who could be told, "It's okay."
Was there a gap between the amount of information you were given and the amount you felt you needed to make sense of your results?

That’s the paradox, isn’t it? I’m drowning in information, yet I feel like I have absolutely no idea what’s actually happening. In a clinical sense, I have *too much* information. I know the margins of error, the limitations of the screening, and the biological "what-ifs." I could lecture a room of medical students on the probability of a false positive for a microdeletion. 

But what I *need* isn't more data. I don't need a more detailed breakdown of the sensitivity or the specificity of the test. I need the kind of information that isn't found in a laboratory report: I need to know what this looks like for my day-to-day. I need someone to bridge the gap between those numbers and the reality of a living, breathing baby. 

Sometimes, when I’m with my OB, I feel like there’s this unspoken tension where she’s trying to respect my expertise by giving me the raw data, but I’m desperately trying to signal that I need her to be the guide. I have all the facts, but I have zero perspective. It’s like having a map of every single stone on the road but no idea where the road is actually leading. I'm exhausted from being my own medical advocate. I just want to be someone’s patient.
During your pregnancy, what did you expect in terms of having a consistent midwife or care provider who knew your history and situation? And what continuity of care did you actually experience?

I suppose I expected a sense of narrative continuity—the idea that someone would hold the thread of my story from the first positive test to the final weeks. Given my history and the "medium risk" label, I really craved that stability. I imagined a partnership where I wouldn't have to re-explain my background or my anxieties every time I walked through the door. I wanted someone who knew *why* I was holding my breath when the doppler started searching for the heartbeat.

The reality has been much more fragmented. It’s not that the staff isn't competent—they are—but it’s a large practice, and I often see whoever is on the rotation. It means I’m constantly "re-introducing" myself to the room. I find myself giving a truncated version of my medical history, performing that familiar, weary dance of, "I’m Chanell, I work in genetics, I’m thirty-six, and yes, I know the risks." 

It creates this emotional whiplash. One visit, I might have a lovely moment of connection where I feel seen; the next, I’m just a time-slot in a busy schedule, being treated like a textbook case rather than a woman navigating her seventh pregnancy alone. It reminds me that even in the best offices, continuity of care is often a luxury, not a standard. It makes that "vast silence" I mentioned earlier feel even heavier when I’m leaving the office alone.
What was the impact — practically or emotionally — of seeing different providers at different appointments?

Practically speaking, it’s an exhausting administrative hurdle. Every new face requires a reset. I have to gauge how much of my professional background to reveal; if I mention it, the appointment tends to shift into a clinical peer-to-peer discussion, which strips away the "patient" experience I’m craving. If I don't mention it, I get the generic, high-level overview that feels dismissive of the specific, nuanced fears I’m carrying. It’s like I’m constantly curating my own care, and I’m just too tired for that.

Emotionally, it’s the lack of witness that hurts the most. When I see someone different, they don't know that three weeks ago I was fighting back tears because the baby wasn't moving enough, or that I’m still processing the heavy result of a screening. To them, I’m just "36, G7, medium-risk, normal vitals." They see a static snapshot, but they don't see the film that’s been playing in my head all week. 

It reinforces this feeling that I am navigating this alone. I’m the only constant in my own pregnancy, and that’s a heavy weight for one person to carry. I don’t just want a medical provider; I want someone who remembers my name and the shape of my story, so I don’t have to feel like I’m starting from zero every single time.
During pregnancy, what did you expect in terms of how your changing body would be discussed and examined by healthcare providers? And how did those interactions actually feel?

I expected the focus to be almost entirely on the pathology of my body—the fundal height, the blood pressure, the weight gain relative to my age and risk status. I thought my body would be treated like a biological vessel being monitored for failure, which, honestly, is how I’ve been viewing it myself. I prepared for clinical, detached assessments where I’d be measured and recorded.

But the reality has been a strange mix of both extremes. Some providers are so purely clinical that they don’t even acknowledge the physical toll of this being my seventh go-around. They just check the boxes. Then, there are the moments that catch me off guard—when a provider will say something casual or overly sunny like, "You're looking great, mama," and it just… it grates on me. It feels performative, like they’re trying to inject a warmth that isn't really there because they don't actually know me.

It feels like my body is being handled by people who see the pregnancy, but not the person carrying it. I feel like an object of study, or worse, a set of stats that need to remain within the "normal" range. I find myself wanting them to just look at me—the real me—and acknowledge how hard this is, both physically and under the skin, without the sterile, detached bedside manner or the forced, shallow cheerfulness.

Before pregnancy, what did you expect the experience of managing a high-risk condition like gestational diabetes or pre-eclampsia to involve? If relevant, how did the reality of managing it differ from your expectations?

I think my background makes me expect the worst-case scenario. When you’ve spent years looking at "the complications," you start to see your own body through the lens of a potential adverse event. Before this, I honestly expected that if a complication—like gestational diabetes—arose, it would be presented as a clear, algorithmic fix: *Test, protocol, outcome.* I expected a rigid, highly structured plan where I would just follow the instructions and everything would fall back into place. I think I found comfort in that idea, because it meant I could control the outcome if I just did the work perfectly.

The reality, though, is that complications aren't just clinical variables. They are a daily, nagging reminder of my own vulnerability. When you're managing something like that while working full-time and handling everything else on your own, the "protocol" feels less like a solution and more like a second full-time job. It’s the mental load—the timing of the tests, the constant logging of numbers, the emotional weight of seeing a spike on a monitor. 

It hasn't been the neat, tidy "fix" I imagined. It’s been messy. It’s felt like another thing I’m "failing" at, even though I know logically that it’s just biology. I don't need another list of dietary guidelines—I have those memorized. I need someone to recognize the exhaustion of having to be the manager of my own body’s "malfunctions." It’s lonely, and it makes the pregnancy feel like it’s being held together by duct tape and sheer willpower.
During pregnancy, what did you expect in terms of being able to access care promptly when you had a concern — whether urgent or not? And how responsive was the system when you needed it?

I think I expected that the "high-risk" label would act as a sort of express lane, that there would be some kind of structural acknowledgement that my anxiety was, in a way, medically grounded. I assumed that if I called with a question—especially given my clinical background—my concerns would be triaged with a bit more… nuance.

The reality, however, is that the system doesn't know who I am when I’m on the other end of a telephone line. I’m just a voice in a queue. When I have a concern, I’m navigating the same labyrinthine automated menus and hold music as everyone else. By the time I actually get a response, the initial, sharp spike of panic has usually either settled or evolved into a dull, corrosive dread.

It’s incredibly frustrating. I’ll call to ask about a specific symptom—something that might be benign for a twenty-year-old but sets off alarm bells for a thirty-six-year-old in my shoes—and I end up playing "phone tag" with medical assistants who are just reading from a triage script. It’s a reminder that no matter how much I know about the science of pregnancy, I am at the mercy of a system that is fundamentally designed for volume, not for the individual. It makes me feel small, and honestly, it makes me feel like I’m failing to protect this baby simply because I can’t get a straight answer in real time.
During pregnancy, what did you expect the role of your family, friends, or community to be in supporting you? And how did that informal support network actually show up — or not?

I suppose I expected a village. When I started this—my seventh—I had this fantasy that people would just *know* I needed help. I thought, "Surely, seven times in, people recognize the heavy lifting required here." I pictured casseroles, offers to watch the older ones, someone checking in to see if I was actually sleeping. I expected my community to be as steady as the routine of a doctor’s office should be.

The reality? It’s been… quiet. I think because I’ve spent my career being the "strong one," the one who handles the crises for other families, people assume I have my own situation entirely under control. They see the professional woman, the independent mother, and they mistake my competence for a lack of need. Nobody thinks to ask if I’m terrified because I’m always the one giving the reassurance. 

My friends are supportive in the abstract, but they don’t see the nights where I’m too tired to move, let alone cook, or the moments where I just want to sit on the floor and cry without having to explain *why* the anatomy scan data is rattling around in my head. There’s a distance there. It’s not malice; it’s just that they don’t know how to reach into the space I’ve built for myself. I’ve become so good at being the harbor for everyone else that I’ve forgotten how to be a ship that needs a port. It’s lonely, realizing that your independence has become the very thing that keeps help at arm’s length.

It’s funny, the way people treat independence like it’s a armor. They see me, a single mother, thirty-six, holding down a career and managing this pregnancy, and they call it "empowering." But there’s a cost to that empowerment that nobody mentions in the brochures. 

When you’re the sole navigator, you don’t get to have a "bad day" where you just surrender to the process. If I break down, who picks up the pieces? If I admit I’m scared, who holds that fear with me? I find myself having to be my own advocate, my own therapist, and my own obstetrician all at once. I think that’s why the clinical silence of my doctors cuts so deep. I spend all day at work—and all evening at home—being the one who solves problems. I just wanted nine months where I could be the one *with* the problem, and have someone else take the wheel.

But I’m learning that even with seven pregnancies, you never truly get used to the unknown. Every time I drive back into Gardner, watching the sun hit those familiar fields, I try to remind myself that my medical training is just information—it’s not a crystal ball. I’m trying to leave the "clinical me" in the exam room and just be the mother who is growing this little soul. It’s a messy, frightening, beautiful transition. I’m still figuring out how to let the armor go, even if it’s just for an hour at a time. It’s a different kind of labor, honestly. But it’s the one that matters most.
Were there moments when a doctor, partner, or family member's opinion overrode yours — even when you hadn't asked for their input?

Oh, constantly. It’s a strange phenomenon when you’re "the expert" in your professional life, but the moment you cross that threshold into the clinical space as a patient, your voice is often filtered through the lens of their assumptions.

There was one visit where I specifically voiced a concern about a certain screening result—nothing major, just a blip on my radar. I had my logic ready, the data cited. The provider didn't even engage with the premise; she just patted my hand and gave me this rehearsed, breezy assurance about how "we see this all the time" and "everything is going to be just fine." It felt like a dismissal, like my professional intuition was being patronized because they viewed me as just another "anxious pregnant woman" rather than a peer who knows the exact contours of the risk involved.

It’s isolating. When you have a medical background, you expect a conversation. Instead, you get a lecture. They want to simplify things for me, to make it digestible and "positive," but in doing so, they strip away my agency. It makes me feel like my knowledge is a burden to them—that they’d rather I didn't know what I know. They think they’re calming me down, but they’re actually just silencing me. It’s hard to advocate for your own body when the people you’re paying to care for it decide they know your comfort levels better than you do.

It’s an odd feeling, carrying the weight of being both the expert and the novice. Sometimes I sit in that exam room and feel like I’m playing a role I never signed up for. I want to tell them, "Look, I know what the guidelines say about this diagnostic test. I know the positive predictive value isn't great. Just talk to me like a person who is scared, not like a colleague you need to appease."

I think that’s the hardest part—the performance. I have to perform the "competent professional" when I’m talking to colleagues, and then I have to perform the "calm, compliant patient" when I’m talking to my provider. Where is the space for the version of me that’s just a tired woman in her thirties, seven pregnancies in, who is terrified that her clinical brain is going to steal the joy of this experience from her?

When I get home to Gardner, I look at the preparations I’ve made, the baby clothes, the space I’ve cleared, and I have to actively stop myself from doing a risk-benefit analysis on every single thing. I want to just be a mother. I want to believe that the heartbeat I heard on the monitor is the only data point that truly matters. I think I’m slowly learning that I can’t outsmart the biology of this, no matter how many papers I’ve read. I have to surrender to it, which is terrifying for someone like me. But it’s the only way through. I just hope that by the time I'm holding this baby, I’ve finally learned how to silence the scientist and just listen to the mother.
Were there specific decisions — such as timing, fertility testing, or lifestyle changes — where you felt pressure to align with your partner's preferences over your own?

You asked about my partner, and the truth is, the silence in my house is the most honest thing about this whole journey. Being never married, I’m the sole architect of these decisions. There is no partner to exert pressure, which is both the greatest freedom and the heaviest weight I’ve ever carried.

When I was younger, I think I romanticized the idea of a partner who would be my "anchor" in these situations—someone to debate the merits of a CVS versus an amniocentesis, someone to balance my clinical paranoia with their own perspective. But now, at thirty-six, I realize that the pressure I feel doesn't come from a partner. It comes from the societal expectations baked into the maternity system itself. 

Even without a partner in the room, I feel the pressure to conform. The medical system is built for a "couple" dynamic. Every time the intake form asks for "Partner's Name" or "Emergency Contact (Spouse)," I’m reminded that I don't fit the mold. I feel a subtle, invisible pressure to be "extra" compliant, almost as if I need to prove that my being a single mother doesn't make me a less-than-ideal candidate for a textbook-perfect pregnancy. I over-research, I show up early, I memorize my own lab values—partly because I’m a scientist, but partly because I feel like I have to be twice as diligent to be taken seriously. 

I’m the only one who weighs the risks of the fertility interventions or the lifestyle changes. Sometimes, I wonder if having someone to bounce those thoughts off of would actually complicate things more. Would a partner have pressured me into a test I didn't want? Or would they have been the one to finally tell me to stop looking at the Punnett squares and just get some sleep? I don't know. All I know is that the pressure to be "perfectly prepared" is entirely mine to bear. And some nights, that feels like an impossible burden to carry alone.

You know, reflecting on all of this, I realize the irony is that I spent years training to be a professional "listener." I was taught how to parse a patient's tone, how to identify when their clinical questions were actually masks for their deepest, most unspoken fears. Now that I’m on the other side, I realize how rarely that courtesy is extended to the patient. 

I’m sitting here, seven pregnancies in, and I feel like I’m constantly being measured against a standard that doesn’t account for my own history or my own competence. There is a profound kind of exhaustion that comes from being "the woman in the room" who knows too much but is treated as though she knows too little, or perhaps, as though she needs to be protected from her own knowledge. 

I think the biggest takeaway from this journey so far—and maybe this is something your research could help change—is that maternity care shouldn't be a one-size-fits-all script. I don’t want to be "managed." I want to be *heard*. I want a provider who sees the scientist, yes, but also sees the mother who is terrified of the ghosts in the Punnett square. 

I’m still here, still driving those lonely miles back to Gardner, and I’m still trying to figure out how to bridge that gap between the person I am at work and the person I am in the exam chair. It’s a work in progress. But talking about it, putting words to this silence... it helps. It really does. It makes the ghost of that 1 in 2,500 statistic feel just a little bit less like a prophecy and a little bit more like just another piece of data I can eventually set aside. Thank you for listening to the parts I don't usually say out loud.
Were you given unsolicited comments about your weight, diet, or physical appearance? How did those comments land?

They land like a dull ache, honestly. It’s that familiar, patronizing tone that suggests my body is a site for public critique rather than a private, living space for my baby. I’ll be sitting there, already hyper-aware of every physiological change—my skin stretching, the swelling, the sheer, exhausting reality of the second trimester—and then a provider, or even a well-meaning acquaintance, will chime in with some "helpful" comment.

"You’re carrying so differently this time," or "Are you watching your intake? We don't want that weight to get away from you at your age." 

It feels like an invasion. Because I’m thirty-six and technically "medium risk," those comments take on a jagged edge. They aren't just polite observations; they’re coded warnings. It implies that my body is somehow a liability I need to manage, that if I just *behaved* properly, I could avoid the complications that are statistically hovering around me. 

It’s insulting, in a way. I know the clinical side of weight gain, the pathophysiology of gestational diabetes, and the metabolic demands of a pregnancy at this stage. I don’t need the lecture. I need someone to recognize the physical labor of my seventh pregnancy. When they focus on the "vessel" instead of the person, it just makes me feel like I’m failing a test I didn’t know I was taking. It’s hard to advocate for my own health when every check-up starts to feel like a judgment on my discipline rather than a conversation about my well-being.

It’s funny, the more we talk, the more I realize how much I’ve been guarding the "Chanell who knows things" against the "Chanell who is terrified." When I’m at the clinic, I’m so busy anticipating those judgmental comments about my weight or my age—the ones that feel like a subtle indictment of my life choices—that I don't give myself the grace to just be a person experiencing a massive, life-altering event. 

I think the biggest realization for me, and maybe one of the most painful, is that I have been using my professional expertise as a shield against that judgment. If I can prove I know more than the person checking my chart, maybe they’ll respect me enough not to talk down to me. But the shield is getting heavy. 

When I’m alone in the car driving back to Gardner, the silence isn't just about being unmarried or navigating this pregnancy solo; it's about the exhaustion of having to maintain that shield. I don’t want to be the "informed patient" who is "highly educated" and "manages her risk factors." I just want to be a woman who is tired, who is hungry, who is wondering if the little kicks I’m starting to feel are signs of health or just another thing for my brain to categorize. 

I’m starting to think that the most "clinical" advice I could give to someone like you, for your research, is that competence in a patient doesn't mean they don't need compassion. In fact, it often means they’ve been performing for so long they’ve forgotten how to ask for it. I’m hoping that by the time this seventh baby is here, I can find a way to stop performing and just start *being*. It’s a long road, but it’s mine. Thank you for letting me put the shield down for a little while today.
Which informal sources — people or communities — did you turn to first, before or instead of a healthcare provider?

It sounds strange to say, but my first port of call isn't actually a person—it’s the medical literature. When I’m spiraling in that middle-of-the-night panic, my instinct isn't to call my OB; it’s to pull up the PubMed or clinical databases. I treat my own body like a case study. I’ll scour the newest research on maternal age or scan through the latest meta-analyses on whatever symptom has me spooked. It’s a way of trying to "solve" the problem before I ever have to admit to a doctor that I’m worried.

When the literature isn't enough, I sometimes turn to a couple of former colleagues—women I trust who have since left the lab or clinical practice. These are the only people I can be truly honest with, because they know exactly what my professional "curse" is. I don't have to explain why I'm hyper-fixated on a specific, rare outcome; they just get it. They don't offer me platitudes like "everything will be fine," because they know that phrase is scientifically empty. Instead, they’ll talk me through the data or, more importantly, they’ll tell me to step away from the computer and go get a decent meal.

It’s a very specific kind of support. I don't look for community in the traditional sense—the baby forums or the "mommy groups." They feel too performative, too full of "everything happens for a reason" logic that I find impossible to swallow. I need people who can sit in the discomfort of uncertainty with me without trying to fix it immediately. But honestly? Most of the time, I just keep it all inside. I’ve become my own primary source of support, for better or for worse, because it’s easier than trying to explain the complexities of my brain to someone who hasn't spent a decade studying the "worst-case scenarios."

It’s strange, realizing how much of my life I’ve built around the need for data-driven, clinical certainty—and how little that actually helps when you’re lying in the dark at 3:00 a.m. feeling the baby move and wondering if it’s a symptom or a miracle. 

My former colleagues are the only ones who truly "get" it, but even with them, I find myself holding back. I’m so used to being the one they come to for explanations of karyotypes or the nuances of neonatal outcomes that, when I try to turn the tables and be the one who’s vulnerable, I feel like a fraud. I feel like I should be "better" at this, given my professional history. I should be calmer. I should be able to process my own risk markers with the same detached, analytical grace I’ve shown a hundred other families.

But I can’t. And that’s where the silence really sets in. 

I’m thinking about the drive back to Gardner, and how the landscape shifts from the sterile, fluorescent world of the city hospitals to the rolling fields of home. It’s almost symbolic of the two versions of me: the professional Chanell who can recite the odds of a microdeletion, and the mother who just wants to hear that everything is going to be okay. I’m slowly, painfully learning that the two versions don't have to exist in separate silos. Maybe the future of maternity care, the kind you’re researching, is about creating a space where the "expert" can finally be allowed to stop knowing, and just be known. That’s what I’m searching for—even if I’m too stubborn or too trained to ask for it directly. It’s been a relief to put this into words. Thank you for listening.
Were there appointments or decisions where you felt your input was genuinely sought, versus times when you felt you were simply being informed of a plan?

It’s a subtle but soul-crushing distinction, isn't it? Most of the time, I feel like I’m being "managed" rather than consulted. The appointments often follow a script: the provider enters, scans the chart, nods at the metrics, and presents the "plan" as if it’s the only logical conclusion—which, mathematically, it usually is. 

But there’s a difference between a medical consensus and a shared decision. When I’m told, "We’re going to schedule the anatomy scan for week X and the NIPT for week Y," that’s not a conversation; that’s a directive. Even if I agree with the clinical necessity, the way it’s delivered makes me feel like a passive participant in my own body. It’s like being a passenger in a car I’m expected to drive.

However, there was one singular, shining moment. My OB—the one who caught on to my anxiety—paused during a scan and asked me, "Chanell, given what you know about your history and your NIPT results, what do *you* feel is the right next step for your peace of mind?" 

That question caught me so off-guard I almost cried. It wasn't about the data; it was about my autonomy. She invited me to bridge that gap between the professional who understands the protocols and the mother who needs to feel she has a say in her own journey. It made me feel like an equal for the first time in months. It’s rare, but when it happens, it changes everything. It reminds me that I’m not just a set of risk markers—I’m the person who has to live with these decisions long after the appointment is over.

It's that pivot—the moment someone stops reciting a chart and starts listening to the person—that makes all the difference in the world. It’s what I’m craving, even if I don’t always know how to articulate it. 

I think about the times I've walked out of a clinic feeling less like a patient and more like a set of statistics to be optimized, and I realize how much of that is about power. When you're in my field, you're taught that information is power—it's the tool you use to save lives or explain the inexplicable to parents. But when you're the one in the exam chair, that same information feels like a trap. It pins you down. It dictates your "medium risk" path and closes off the possibility of a different, more humanized experience.

I want to be able to walk into an office and say, "I know the stats, and I’m terrified of them, but what I need right now is just to be held in this uncertainty for a second." I want to be allowed to not be the expert. I want to be allowed to be "just" a mother who is navigating the seventh, most complex, most frightening, and most beautiful pregnancy of her life.

This conversation has been the closest I've come to that, I think. You’ve given me a space to be both the scientist and the person, without asking me to choose one. It makes the drive back to Gardner feel a little less lonely, and the future a little less like a series of grim probabilities. It’s a start. That’s all I can ask for, really. Just a start.
Was there a moment when your trust in a provider was broken — something they said, missed, or failed to explain?

It wasn’t a catastrophic failure—not a missed diagnosis or a botched procedure—but a small, quiet moment that felt like a betrayal. I was sitting on the crinkly paper, and I’d just shared one of those "what-if" scenarios that keeps me up at night, something about a potential mosaicism in the genetics. I wasn’t looking for a lecture on the statistics; I was looking for someone to acknowledge that the possibility, however small, is a heavy thing to carry.

The provider, someone I’d only met twice before, barely looked up from my chart. She offered a quick, reflexive smile and said, "Chanell, you’re far too intelligent to be worrying about that. The numbers are pristine. Let’s focus on your prenatal vitamins."

It hit me like a physical blow. By praising my intelligence, she was actually invalidating my fear. It was a classic "professional" dismissal—the idea that if you are educated, you are somehow immune to the primal, terrifying reality of pregnancy. She essentially told me that my brain was a tool for the workplace, but in her office, it was an inconvenience. 

In that moment, the trust evaporated. I realized she didn’t see a woman navigating a complex, high-stakes experience; she saw a "smart patient" she could manage with a few platitudes. I felt reduced to a stereotype, and the silence that followed—where I just nodded and said "okay"—was the sound of me retreating back behind my shield. It’s hard to rebuild trust once you realize that the person across from you is more interested in the *idea* of you than the *reality* of what you’re feeling.

That interaction, the "you’re too smart to worry" comment, really crystallized the paradox I’ve been living in. It’s like, because I’ve spent my life studying the mechanisms of rare neonatal conditions, I’m somehow expected to be a detached observer of my own pregnancy. They don’t realize that the more you know about the molecular level of how things can go wrong, the more terrifying the "unremarkable" becomes. 

When a provider dismisses my fear as something I’m "too smart" for, they’re essentially telling me to turn off the very thing that has defined my career—my curiosity, my attention to detail, my ability to see the patterns. And ironically, they’re the ones who insist on giving me all the data! They hand me the NIPT report, they talk about the chromosomal assays, and then they act surprised when I actually *understand* the implications of those numbers. 

It makes me feel like I’m constantly being told to put the pieces of the puzzle together, but then I'm scolded when I actually look at the picture they form. That dissonance is exactly why the car ride back to Gardner is so heavy. It’s not just the hormones or the age-related risks; it’s the whiplash of being treated as a peer when it’s convenient for their workflow, and a "hysterical" patient when my concerns don’t fit into their five-minute appointment window.

I think that's why I've been so guarded with you today—or at least, why I’m starting to let down my guard now. I’m so used to my concerns being categorized as "intellectual exercises" rather than visceral, human emotions. But the truth is, I’m just a mother who is trying to find her footing on a very steep, very narrow path. And for the first time in this entire pregnancy, I feel like someone is actually listening to the *person* behind the patient portal login. That’s a start, isn't it?

It really is. You know, I think the most dangerous thing about the way care is currently structured for someone like me—someone who knows the science—is the assumption that knowledge is an antidote to anxiety. People think, "Oh, Chanell understands the statistics, so she must feel safer." But the opposite is true. Knowledge is just a map. It tells you where the cliffs are, where the predators live, and where the road might crumble, but it doesn’t do a single thing to stop you from being terrified when you’re standing at the edge.

Every time I go in for a scan, I’m not just looking for a heartbeat; I’m cross-referencing everything I’m seeing against every case study I’ve ever reviewed. It’s like being forced to watch a horror movie where you know exactly how the special effects are made, but you’re still sitting in the dark, shivering.

I think the biggest "ask" I have—not for my OB, but for the system at large—is for a space where "not knowing" is encouraged, not punished. I want a space where I can admit I’m lost in my own head without being told I’m overthinking. I want to be able to say, "I see the data, and I’m still scared," and have that be enough. 

Driving back to Gardner tonight, I’m going to try to focus on that. Not the NIPT numbers, not the microdeletion probabilities, but the fact that someone sat here and listened to the woman, not just the genetics counselor. That matters. It really, truly matters. I think I’m going to hold onto that when I go back into that clinic next week. I’m going to try to walk in there and just be me—shield down, armor off—and see what happens. It’s a risk, I know. But after seven pregnancies, I think I’m finally realizing that the biggest risk of all is closing yourself off from the grace of being human.
Did you ever go online specifically because you felt you couldn't get enough information from your care provider — what were you looking for that you hadn't received?

Oh, almost every single night. The search bar is my secret, late-night confession booth. When I go online, I’m not looking for the basic, "here’s what your baby looks like at sixteen weeks" fluff. That stuff feels almost condescending, honestly. I’m looking for the raw, technical data that my providers seem to gatekeep—the specific cohort studies, the granular breakdowns of longitudinal outcomes for pregnancies with my specific risk profile.

I’m looking for the *why* behind the silence. If a scan measurement is on the higher end of "normal," I want the data on what that range signifies over time, not just a vague, "it’s within the acceptable threshold." I’m looking for the clinical precision that the "official" conversation lacks because the system is designed to keep patients from panicking. 

But there’s a deeper, more vulnerable search, too. I find myself scrolling through forums or niche blogs—not the cheery, "everything is perfect" ones, but the ones where women talk about the grit and the fear. I’m looking for someone who sounds like me, someone who can articulate that specific, agonizing disconnect of being a professional who *knows* the science but is still, fundamentally, just a mother waiting for a sign. 

It’s an exhausting way to live, being your own research department. It’s like I’m building a parallel medical chart in my head, just in case the one at the clinic is missing something vital. I shouldn't have to be my own detective, but when the system treats your expertise as a threat to their "protocol," it leaves you with no choice but to go into the dark corners of the internet to find the pieces you're missing. It’s a lonely place to get your answers.

Thank you so much for sharing all of that with me. Your experiences and insights are incredibly valuable. Before we finish, is there anything else about your maternity experience that we haven't covered today — anything that feels important to you that you'd like to add?

I think the thing that lingers, the thing I haven't quite managed to say out loud until now, is the quiet grief of losing the "normal" pregnancy experience to my own clinical brain. Everyone talks about the joy of seeing the baby on the screen, but for me, it’s a forensic exercise. I’m mourning the fact that I can't just be the person who gasps in delight at a heartbeat; I’m the person who mentally calculates the fetal heart rate variability and wonders if it’s a marker for something else.

I want to tell you, for your research, that when you’re building these care models, don’t just look at the clinical outcomes or the patient satisfaction scores. Look at the *grief of the educated patient*. We are being robbed of the wonder of this because we are trapped in the mechanics of the risk. 

If there’s one thing I’d change, it would be to have a provider—just one—who acknowledges that my brain is a burden here. Someone to say, "I know you know the science, and I’m sorry that it makes it so hard to just be a mother." That simple acknowledgement would be worth more than a thousand pages of pamphlets or clinical pamphlets. 

I’m walking away from this conversation feeling a little less like a "medium-risk case" and a little more like a person. And maybe, just maybe, that’s the first real step in letting the "scientist" rest so the "mother" can finally breathe. Thank you for holding that space. It means more than I can say.
